- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- foundation-creator: Use this skill when the user wants to write, draft, update, or revise a top-level foundation document for a company, product, or new primitive. This document captures thesis, mission, boundaries, actor model, surfaces, strategic bets, and open questions without collapsing ambiguity into implementation decisions. Applies when the user is still defining what the system is, what it is not, and what long-term direction it implies. Does NOT apply to concrete service specifications in `SPEC.md`, implementation plans, PRDs, or roadmap execution docs.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- spec-creator: Use this skill when the user wants to write, draft, update, or revise a top-level SPEC.md service specification — the language-agnostic behavioral spec that sits at a project's root and describes what a long-running service or automation does (problem statement, goals/non-goals, components, domain model). Applies when the user says things like "write a spec for X", "draft a specification", "update SPEC.md to add Y", "revise the non-goals", "add a new component to the spec", or hands over notes and asks for them to be turned into a formal service spec. Also applies when the user references an existing SPEC.md and wants edits, even if they don't explicitly use the word "spec". Covers both greenfield creation and targeted updates of an existing file. Does NOT apply to per-topic specs under thoughts/shared/specs/ (those are produced by create_ralph_topics from a JTBD), nor to JTBDs, implementation plans, or PRDs.
- lightfast-aeo: Use when authoring or reviewing any Lightfast content destined for AI engine retrieval —
release notes, blog posts, docs pages, integration pages, anything published to the
public site. Triggers on requests to write, draft, rewrite, or audit prose that should
be cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Codex, Google AI Overviews, or other answer engines,
even when the user doesn't say "AEO", "citations", or "answer engine". Per-content-type
skills compose this for voice and section ordering — load this one too whenever they
activate.
- lightfast-changelog: Write and review AEO-optimized changelog entries for Lightfast. Triggers when the user asks
to write a changelog entry, review changelog content, or asks about changelog structure,
AEO optimization, or release notes formatting.
- lightfast-sdk: Use Lightfast tools to search across team knowledge and interact with connected providers
(GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Vercel, Apollo). Triggers when the user asks about errors, issues,
PRs, deployments, or wants to search across their dev tools. Also triggers proactively when
investigating bugs, reviewing code, or checking deployment status.
- lightfast-debug: General-purpose Lightfast debugging workflow. Triggers when the user asks to debug,
investigate, trace, reproduce, inspect, or diagnose behavior across the app, platform,
Inngest workflows, database state, connected providers, or observability tooling. Use as
the broad entrypoint when no narrower skill is clearly better, when the failing surface is
unclear, or when a bug may cross multiple systems. Prefer `lightfast-db`,
`lightfast-inngest`, or `lightfast-sdk` for direct single-surface requests that clearly map
to one of those tools.
- lightfast-inngest: Inspect Inngest function runs, debug failures, send events, and invoke functions via the
local Inngest dev server MCP. Triggers when the user asks about background jobs, workflow
runs, failed steps, event delivery, or wants to invoke/test Inngest functions.
- lightfast-clerk: Provision Clerk test users, sign in via the browser, mint JWTs, and tear
everything down — for local-dev tRPC / desktop / API testing. Triggers when
the user wants to call Lightfast tRPC procedures with a real auth token,
test desktop sign-in flows, or set up / clean up test users in Clerk dev.
Dev-only: refuses to run against pk_live_ keys or non-localhost URLs.
- lightfast-db: Query and inspect the Postgres database schema and data. Triggers when the user asks about
tables, columns, rows, database schema, wants to run a SELECT query, or needs to understand
the data model. Read-only — cannot modify data.
- turborepo: Turborepo monorepo build system guidance. Triggers on: turbo.json, task pipelines,
dependsOn, caching, remote cache, the "turbo" CLI, --filter, --affected, CI optimization, environment
variables, internal packages, monorepo structure/best practices, and boundaries.

Use when user: configures tasks/workflows/pipelines, creates packages, sets up
monorepo, shares code between apps, runs changed/affected packages, debugs cache,
or has apps/packages directories.
- implement_plan: Implement technical plans from thoughts/shared/plans with verification
- create_plan: Create detailed implementation plans through interactive research and iteration
- validate_plan: Validate implementation against plan, verify success criteria, identify issues
- commit: Create git commits with user approval and no Claude attribution
- oneshot_merge: Commit changes, create PR, and merge into main in one shot
- improve_plan: Adversarial review of implementation plans — find weaknesses, kill code smells, improve DX
- research_codebase: Document codebase as-is with thoughts directory for historical context
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Now let me research the latest Next.js 16 features relevant to agent development.

input.description: Research Next 16 features for agent dev
input.prompt:
Research the latest Next.js 16 (and late 15.x) features that are most relevant for AI agent / LLM application development. I need a concise reference of what's new and what the best-practice defaults are.

Focus areas:
1. **Streaming & long-running requests**: `maxDuration`, streaming best practices, fluid compute, `after()` API (formerly `unstable_after`), SSE vs Response streams, keep-alive patterns.
2. **Caching for AI apps**: the new `'use cache'` directive, `cacheLife`, `cacheTag`, `cacheComponents` flag (formerly "dynamic IO"), Partial Prerendering status in 16, when to opt out (AI output is non-deterministic).
3. **Runtime choices**: Node middleware runtime (stable in 16?), Edge vs Node, nodejs_compat.
4. **Turbopack stability in 16** (build + dev), production readiness, known regressions.
5. **Auth/error helpers**: `forbidden()` and `unauthorized()` helpers, `onRequestError`, instrumentation-client.
6. **Async request APIs**: `cookies()`, `headers()`, `params`, `searchParams` all became Promises in 15 — anything changed in 16?
7. **React Compiler status in Next 16**: stable? gotchas?
8. **Typed routes** (`typedRoutes`): stable?
9. **Anything specific for tRPC + AI streaming** patterns the Next team recommends.
10. **Bundle analyzer / `optimizePackageImports`** — any changes in how these work in 16.
11. **`revalidateTag`, `revalidatePath`** — still the way, or superseded by `'use cache'` + `cacheTag`?

## Analysis: Next.js 16 and late 15.x — AI/LLM Application Reference

### Summary

Next.js 16 (released October 2025, current stable is 16.2 as of March 2026) is a significant architectural release. Its three headline changes are: Turbopack becoming the default bundler, Cache Components (the unified replacement for `dynamicIO` + `experimental.ppr`) reaching production status, and `middleware.ts` being replaced by `proxy.ts` with a Node.js-only runtime. The 16.2 point release added specific AI-agent tooling (`AGENTS.md`, `next-browser`, browser-log forwarding), a ~400% faster dev startup, and 25-60% faster RSC rendering from a React upstream optimization.

---

### Key Findings

#### 1. Streaming, maxDuration, after(), and Fluid Compute

**Confidence**: High

`after()` became stable in Next.js 15.1 (introduced as `unstable_after` in 15.0-RC). It runs callbacks after the response is flushed, bounded by the route's `maxDuration`. On serverless platforms it relies on `waitUntil()`; on self-hosted Node it works natively. Inside `after()` callbacks within Server Components, you cannot call `cookies()` or `headers()` — you must read those values before the callback and close over them. For Route Handlers and Server Functions, direct `cookies()`/`headers()` calls inside `after()` are fine. `maxDuration` is set via `export const maxDuration = N` in any route segment config and remains the standard signal to hosting platforms to extend function lifetime. Fluid Compute is a Vercel-platform concept (not a Next.js config flag) that amortizes cold starts across concurrent streaming requests on a single function instance — the correct Next.js pairing is `maxDuration` + `Response` streams from a Route Handler.

For AI streaming the recommended pattern is: use a Route Handler returning a `ReadableStream` or Vercel AI SDK `StreamingTextResponse`; wrap in `Suspense` when consuming from RSC. `after()` is the right hook for post-stream logging (analytics, token accounting) without blocking TTFB. The Inngest blog (March 2026) confirms that Inngest's own Durable Endpoints explicitly do not support streaming — for streaming AI, stay on plain Route Handlers.

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/after` (official docs), `inngest.com/blog/2026-03-09-how-to-fix-nextjs-ai-route-failures`

---

#### 2. Caching for AI Apps — use cache, cacheLife, cacheTag, cacheComponents, PPR

**Confidence**: High

`'use cache'` is stable in 16 (it was experimental in 15). It is enabled by setting `cacheComponents: true` in `next.config.ts` — this single flag replaces and unifies the former `experimental.dynamicIO`, `experimental.ppr`, and `experimental.useCache` flags, all of which are removed in 16. PPR is no longer a separate concept: when `cacheComponents` is enabled, PPR is the default rendering model for every route. The old `export const experimental_ppr = true` segment config is removed. With `cacheComponents` enabled, the `dynamic`, `dynamicParams`, `revalidate`, and `fetchCache` segment config exports are also removed.

For AI output: do not put LLM responses inside `'use cache'` — LLM responses are non-deterministic and personalised. The correct pattern is: wrap the LLM fetch in `<Suspense>` without `'use cache'`, so it streams at request time. Only cache upstream context (user profile, retrieved documents, system prompts) that is stable. The default `cacheLife` profile is `{ stale: 5min, revalidate: 15min, expire: never }`. Named profiles like `'hours'`, `'days'`, `'max'` are available. Custom profiles can be defined in `next.config`.

`'use cache'` at the data function level auto-generates cache keys from all arguments and closed-over outer-scope values. Arguments must be serializable (no class instances, no `Symbol`, no `URL`). In serverless environments the runtime LRU cache does not persist across invocations — only build-time cache is reliable without a `use cache: remote` backend (Redis/KV).

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/blog/next-16`, `nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache`, `nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents`, `nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/caching`

---

#### 3. Runtime Choices — proxy.ts (Node.js) vs middleware.ts (Edge, deprecated)

**Confidence**: High, with one noted open issue

`proxy.ts` is the new name for `middleware.ts` in 16.0 and runs exclusively on the Node.js runtime. Rename the file and rename the exported function from `default` to `proxy`. The old `middleware.ts` still works but is deprecated and will be removed in a future version — it remains the only path for Edge-runtime request interception. The docs note the naming change clarifies that `proxy.ts` is a network-layer concern, not application logic. There is an open GitHub issue (Oct 2025, #85261) confirming that `onRequestError` in `instrumentation.ts` does not currently receive errors thrown from `proxy.ts` — this is an unresolved bug. The `onRequestError` `context.routeType` field was renamed from `'middleware'` to `'proxy'` in a breaking change (PR #85006). For AI apps running expensive inference in route handlers, prefer the Node.js runtime (`export const runtime = 'nodejs'` or simply omit the export since Node.js is the default) — the Edge runtime lacks Node.js APIs needed by most AI SDKs.

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/blog/next-16`, GitHub issue #85261, GitHub PR #85006, GitHub issue #85344

---

**Confidence**: High

Turbopack is the default bundler for both `next dev` and `next build` starting in 16.0. No configuration needed. You can opt back to webpack with `next dev --webpack` or `next build --webpack`. By 16.0, more than 50% of dev sessions and 20% of production builds on 15.3+ were already on Turbopack. Filesystem caching (`turbopackFileSystemCacheForDev`) is enabled by default in dev; the build equivalent (`turbopackFileSystemCacheForBuild`) is opt-in (beta). Starting 16.2, over 200 Turbopack bug fixes shipped. Known parity gaps: webpack plugins are not supported (webpack loaders are); automatic root-layout creation is not supported. Babel configs are now automatically detected and used (previously caused a hard error). The 16.2 dedicated Turbopack post reports Server Fast Refresh and tree-shaking improvements shipped in that release.

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/blog/next-16`, `nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/turbopack`, `nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-turbopack`

---

#### 5. Auth/Error Helpers — forbidden(), unauthorized(), onRequestError, instrumentation-client

**Confidence**: Medium

`forbidden()` and `unauthorized()` (from `next/navigation`) were introduced in 15.1 and remain experimental in 16 behind `experimental.authInterrupts: true`. They are not recommended for production. They throw special errors that render `forbidden.js` / `unauthorized.js` file-convention pages (403/401). They cannot be called from the root layout. `onRequestError` in `instrumentation.ts` is the hook for catching unhandled errors across all route types — the `context.routeType` value was renamed from `'middleware'` to `'proxy'` in 16 (breaking change). There is a known open issue that `onRequestError` does not receive proxy errors at all in 16. `instrumentation-client.ts` (introduced in 15.x for client-side instrumentation) does not appear in the 16.0 or 16.2 release notes, suggesting no changes. 16.2 added `unstable_catchError()` and `unstable_retry()` for component-level error boundaries.

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/forbidden`, GitHub issue #85261, `nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2`

---

#### 6. Async Request APIs — cookies(), headers(), params, searchParams

**Confidence**: High

The codemod `npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest` handles the migration. Pattern: `const cookieStore = await cookies()`, `const headersList = await headers()`, `const { id } = await params`. There are no new changes in 16 beyond making the removal hard.

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/blog/next-16` (breaking changes table), `nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/caching`

---

#### 7. React Compiler Status in Next.js 16

**Confidence**: High

React Compiler 1.0 shipped, and built-in Next.js support for it graduated from `experimental.reactCompiler` to the top-level `reactCompiler: true` config key. It is stable but not enabled by default — the team is still collecting build performance data across project types. Expect higher compile times in dev and build because the Compiler uses Babel. The Compiler relies on code following the Rules of React; it falls back gracefully for violations rather than breaking. Install `babel-plugin-react-compiler@latest` alongside enabling the config.

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/blog/next-16`

---

#### 8. Typed Routes

**Confidence**: High

`typedRoutes` has been promoted from `experimental.typedRoutes` to the stable top-level `typedRoutes: true` config key. The docs explicitly note: "This option has been marked as stable, so you should use `typedRoutes` instead of `experimental.typedRoutes`." Requires TypeScript.

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/typedRoutes`

---

**Confidence**: Medium (no official Next.js team guidance found; synthesis from community + tRPC docs)

The Next.js team does not publish specific tRPC guidance. The community consensus for tRPC v11 + App Router is: call the tRPC router directly from Server Components via `caller` (zero HTTP overhead, same auth context), use HTTP transport for Client Component subscriptions. For AI streaming specifically, tRPC subscriptions via WebSockets or SSE are the typed-safe path but add operational complexity. The practical production pattern is simpler: use a plain Next.js Route Handler returning a `ReadableStream` (or Vercel AI SDK `StreamingTextResponse`) for the stream itself, and use tRPC procedures for non-streaming structured calls (auth checks, saving messages, token metering). **Sources**: `dev.to/whoffagents/trpc-v11-nextjs-app-router` (community), `inngest.com/blog` (authoritative for Inngest patterns)

---

**Confidence**: Medium

A curated set of packages is optimized by default (lucide-react, date-fns, lodash-es, @mui/material, recharts, react-icons/\*, @effect/\*, and ~20 more). There is no change noted in the 16.0 or 16.2 release notes. The `@next/bundle-analyzer` package continues to work alongside Turbopack (it wraps the build output, not the bundler itself).

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/optimizePackageImports`

**Confidence**: High

10. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/turbopack` — Official docs.
11. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config` — Official docs.
12. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/optimizePackageImports` — Official docs.

There is no change noted in the 16.0 or 16.2 release notes. The `@next/bundle-analyzer` package continues to work alongside Turbopack (it wraps the build output, not the bundler itself).

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/optimizePackageImports`

---

#### 11. revalidateTag vs use cache + cacheTag

**Confidence**: High

Both remain valid but serve different semantics. `revalidateTag(tag, profile)` now requires a second argument (a `cacheLife` profile name or `{ expire: number }`) — the single-argument form is deprecated in 16. This enables stale-while-revalidate: the user gets cached data immediately while Next.js revalidates in the background. `updateTag(tag)` (new in 16, Server Actions only) provides read-your-writes semantics: it expires the cache and reads fresh data within the same request — use this for mutations where the user must see their own changes immediately. `refresh()` (new in 16, Server Actions only) refreshes only uncached data without touching the cache at all. For AI apps where output is non-deterministic, the recommended pattern is to not cache LLM output (`'use cache'` is for deterministic, reusable data), use `updateTag` in the Server Action that saves a new AI response, and let `revalidateTag('max')` handle background-refreshing any upstream context that is cacheable.

**Sources**: `nextjs.org/blog/next-16` (Improved Caching APIs section), `nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache`

---

### Source Chain

1. `https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16` — Official docs (Next.js team), October 2025. Primary source for all 16.0 features.
2. `https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2` — Official docs (Next.js team), March 2026. Primary source for 16.2 features.
3. `https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai` — Official docs (Next.js team), March 2026. Authoritative for AI agent tooling.
4. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache` — Official docs, crawled April 2026.
5. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/caching` — Official docs, crawled April 2026.
6. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents` — Official docs.
7. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/after` — Official docs.
8. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/forbidden` — Official docs (experimental).
9. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/typedRoutes` — Official docs.
10. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/turbopack` — Official docs.
11. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config` — Official docs.
12. `https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/optimizePackageImports` — Official docs.
13. `https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/85261` — Maintainer-tracked issue, October 2025. `onRequestError` bug.
14. `https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/85006` — Merged PR, October 2025. `onRequestError` routeType rename.
15. `https://www.inngest.com/blog/2026-03-09-how-to-fix-nextjs-ai-route-failures` — Community (Inngest), March 2026. Authoritative for Inngest-specific patterns.

---

### Conflicts

- `proxy.ts` runtime: The official 16.0 blog post states `proxy.ts` "runs on the Node.js runtime." GitHub issue #85344 (Oct 2025) reports inconsistency in the docs between Edge and Node.js for proxy. The blog is more authoritative; treat Node.js as the canonical runtime for `proxy.ts` and Edge as the path only available via the deprecated `middleware.ts`.
- `forbidden()`/`unauthorized()`: The functions were noted in some community posts as "stable in 16." The official docs page still carries the experimental warning as of the crawl date. Official docs win — treat as experimental.

### Gaps

- No official Next.js team guidance on tRPC + streaming AI — the tRPC finding is synthesised from community posts.
- `instrumentation-client.ts` behaviour in 16 not covered in release notes; assumed unchanged.
- `optimizePackageImports` stability status is ambiguous — the docs say experimental but there is no mention of changes or stabilisation in 16.0 or 16.2.
- The `onRequestError` / `proxy.ts` bug (issue #85261) was open as of October 2025; resolution status unknown.
- No data on whether `nodejs_compat` flag (Cloudflare Workers compatibility layer) has changed in 16.

### Local Relevance

This repo uses tRPC (`@api/app` layer) and Inngest. The Inngest blog explicitly confirms Durable Endpoints do not support streaming, which matches the existing architecture where Inngest handles background workflows and Next.js Route Handlers handle streaming responses. The `cacheComponents` flag is not yet present in this codebase (still on 15.x patterns based on the git branch name). The `proxy.ts` migration from `middleware.ts` would be relevant whenever this repo upgrades to 16. The `after()` API (stable since 15.1) is safe to use today for post-response analytics logging.

Here's the assessment. Both apps are on Next **16.2.4** / React **19.2.5** / Turbopack-default, which is current as of April 2026. Overall you're in good shape — most Next 16 best practices are already in place. A few real gaps and deferred opportunities.

## What's already good
- **Turbopack** for `dev` + `build` (default in 16 ✓).
- **React Compiler** gated to `VERCEL` so local dev stays fast — correct tradeoff (`vendor/next/src/config.ts:19`).
- **`typedRoutes: true`** (stable form) on app (`apps/app/next.config.ts:13`).
- **`proxy.ts` convention** adopted on app — `apps/app/src/proxy.ts` is the Next 16 name for the old `middleware.ts`, running on Node so Clerk/JWKS works.
- **`after()`** already in use for post-response work (`apps/app/src/app/(early-access)/_actions/early-access.ts:203`).
- **Streaming hygiene** on `apps/app/src/app/api/gateway/stream/route.ts`: `runtime="nodejs"`, `dynamic="force-dynamic"`, `maxDuration=300`, `Last-Event-ID` catch-up, heartbeats, `request.signal` abort cleanup. This is the right pattern for agent/LLM SSE.
- **Runtime split** is sensible: `nodejs` for tRPC/crypto/LLM, `edge` for health + Inngest handler, with explicit comment about Octokit needing Node (`apps/app/src/app/(trpc)/api/trpc/[trpc]/route.ts:7`).
- **`instrumentation.ts` + `instrumentation-client.ts`** wired with `onRequestError`, `captureRouterTransitionStart`, and lazy replay/feedback loading — textbook.
- **`optimizePackageImports`** used, `withBundleAnalyzer` gated behind `ANALYZE=true`.
- **`params`/`searchParams`** correctly awaited (Next 15+ async API) e.g. `apps/platform/src/app/api/ingest/[provider]/route.ts:55`.

## Real gaps (worth fixing)

1. **`apps/platform/src/middleware.ts` is deprecated in Next 16.** Rename to `proxy.ts` and change the default-exported function name to `proxy`. Matches the pattern already used in `apps/app/src/proxy.ts`. 5-minute change.

2. **Platform is missing `typedRoutes: true`** in `apps/platform/next.config.ts`. Free type safety on `<Link>` / `router.push()`. Platform has few pages but cost is zero.

3. **`dynamic = "force-dynamic"` scattered on 8 authenticated pages.** With Clerk auth + tRPC prefetch, Next 16 infers dynamic automatically; the explicit export is usually dead weight. Also blocks any future migration to `cacheComponents` (16 removes those segment exports when it's on). Not urgent, but good to audit next time you touch those pages.

## Deferred / optional (understand the tradeoff before flipping)

4. **`cacheComponents: true`** (the unified 16 replacement for `dynamicIO` + `experimental.ppr`). Enables `'use cache'` + `<Suspense>` PPR. **Likely low ROI for this repo** because app pages are authenticated dashboards (mostly dynamic), and LLM output should never be cached. Only worth enabling if you want to `'use cache'` stable context (org metadata, user profile, repo list) and stream the rest. If you do flip it, the `dynamic`/`revalidate` segment exports must go.

5. **`maxDuration = 300` (5 min) is the Vercel Pro streaming ceiling.** Fine for short LLM turns; agent runs that exceed this are correctly delegated to Inngest. Confirm **Fluid Compute is enabled on the Vercel project** — it's a dashboard toggle, not a config flag, and is the single biggest win for concurrent AI streaming (amortizes cold starts across in-flight streams).

6. **Consider `after()` in the SSE route** (`apps/app/src/app/api/gateway/stream/route.ts`) for per-stream analytics / token accounting so it doesn't block `controller.close()`.

7. **Known Next 16 bug**: `onRequestError` doesn't catch errors thrown from `proxy.ts` (upstream issue vercel/next.js#85261). Clerk middleware errors may silently miss Sentry until fixed. Worth a watch.

8. **`forbidden()` / `unauthorized()`** are still behind `experimental.authInterrupts` in 16 — don't adopt for production auth flows yet; stick with the current redirect pattern in `proxy.ts`.

## Summary
Quick wins: rename `platform/src/middleware.ts` → `proxy.ts` and add `typedRoutes: true` to `platform/next.config.ts`. Everything else is either already done or a deferred architectural choice — and your agent-streaming patterns (SSE + Last-Event-ID + abort cleanup + Inngest offload for long work) are the right shape for Next 16 + Vercel Fluid Compute.

I have everything I need from the primary sources. Here is the full analysis.

---

## Analysis: Next.js 16.2 Browser Log Forwarding

### Summary

Next.js 16.2 (released 2026-03-18) promoted browser-to-terminal log forwarding from `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal` (introduced in v15.4.0) to a stable, non-experimental `logging.browserToTerminal` key. It is purely a config-flag feature with no client-side setup required. The companion `AGENTS.md` convention and the separate `@vercel/next-browser` CLI are distinct features shipped in the same release.

---

### Key Findings

#### 1. Exact config flag name and shape

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: The stable flag is `logging.browserToTerminal`, not `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal`. The experimental name existed from v15.4.0 and was moved to stable in v16.2.0.
- **Quote**: "v16.2.0 — `browserToTerminal` added (moved from `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal`)" — https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/browserDebugInfoInTerminal

Minimal config snippet (verbatim from official docs):

```ts
const nextConfig = {
  logging: {
    browserToTerminal: true,
    // 'error' — errors only (default)
    // 'warn'  — warnings and errors
    // true    — all console output
    // false   — disabled
  },
};

export default nextConfig;
```

#### 2. What gets forwarded

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: `console.log`, `console.info`, `console.warn`, `console.error` are all forwarded when set to `true`. Each log line includes source location (file path and line number), e.g. `[browser] Hello World (app/page.tsx:8:17)`. The docs do not list uncaught exception forwarding or source-mapped stack traces as a separate capability — source location is line/column, not a full stack trace.
- **Quote**: "Forward browser console logs (such as `console.log`, `console.warn`, `console.error`) to the terminal during development. [...] browser logs include source location information (file path and line number) by default." — https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/browserDebugInfoInTerminal

Accepted values:

| Value | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `'error'` | Errors only |
| `'warn'` | Warnings and errors |
| `true` | All console output (log, info, warn, error) |
| `false` | Disabled |

The docs table says `'warn'` is the default ("Forward only warnings and errors, by default"), but the AI blog post says only errors are forwarded by default — this is a minor conflict between the two official sources (see Conflicts section).

#### 3. Client-side setup required

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: No client-side setup is required. It is purely a `next.config.ts` flag. Next.js injects the forwarding mechanism automatically.
- **Quote**: "Next.js now forwards browser errors to the terminal by default during development, so you can see client-side errors without switching to the browser console." — https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai

#### 4. Relationship to `instrumentation-client.ts` / Sentry `captureConsoleIntegration`

- **Confidence**: Uncertain
- **Claim**: The official docs and blog post make no mention of `instrumentation-client.ts` or Sentry's `captureConsoleIntegration` interaction. There is no documented deduplication, conflict, or composition story. This is an open gap — both mechanisms intercept `console.*` at the client level, so double-firing or ordering issues are plausible but unverified against source code.
- **Provenance**: Not addressed in https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai or the logging docs reference page. Would require reading Next.js source or Sentry SDK source to resolve.

#### 5. AGENTS.md convention

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: `create-next-app` now scaffolds an `AGENTS.md` file by default in 16.2. It is a short directive telling agents to read docs from `node_modules/next/dist/docs/` before writing code. The Next.js npm package bundles full docs as Markdown. `CLAUDE.md` uses `@AGENTS.md` to include it as context for Claude Code. This is a separate feature from browser log forwarding — they are co-released but independent.
- **Quote**: "`create-next-app` now includes an `AGENTS.md` file by default, giving AI coding agents access to version-matched Next.js documentation from the start of your project." — https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai
- **Provenance**: Based on Vercel's own research showing 100% eval pass rate with bundled docs vs 79% with skill-based retrieval.

#### 6. Turbopack dependency / dev-only vs production

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: Browser log forwarding is dev-only. The docs explicitly say "during development" throughout. There is no Turbopack requirement stated — it lives under the `logging` key which is a general dev config. The `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal` predecessor similarly applied to dev mode. ## Analysis: Next.js 16.2 Browser Log Forwarding

### Summary

Next.js 16.2 (released 2026-03-18) promoted browser-to-terminal log forwarding from `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal` (introduced in v15.4.0) to a stable, non-experimental `logging.browserToTerminal` key. It is purely a config-flag feature with no client-side setup required. The companion `AGENTS.md` convention and the separate `@vercel/next-browser` CLI are distinct features shipped in the same release.

---

### Key Findings

#### 1. Exact config flag name and shape

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: The stable flag is `logging.browserToTerminal`, not `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal`. The experimental name existed from v15.4.0 and was moved to stable in v16.2.0.
- **Quote**: "v16.2.0 — `browserToTerminal` added (moved from `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal`)" — https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/browserDebugInfoInTerminal

Minimal config snippet (verbatim from official docs):

```ts
const nextConfig = {
  logging: {
    browserToTerminal: true,
    // 'error' — errors only (default)
    // 'warn'  — warnings and errors
    // true    — all console output
    // false   — disabled
  },
};

export default nextConfig;
```

#### 2. What gets forwarded

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: `console.log`, `console.info`, `console.warn`, `console.error` are all forwarded when set to `true`. Each log line includes source location (file path and line number), e.g. `[browser] Hello World (app/page.tsx:8:17)`. The docs do not list uncaught exception forwarding or source-mapped stack traces as a separate capability — source location is line/column, not a full stack trace.
- **Quote**: "Forward browser console logs (such as `console.log`, `console.warn`, `console.error`) to the terminal during development. [...] browser logs include source location information (file path and line number) by default." — https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/browserDebugInfoInTerminal

Accepted values:

| Value | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `'error'` | Errors only |
| `'warn'` | Warnings and errors |
| `true` | All console output (log, info, warn, error) |
| `false` | Disabled |

The docs table says `'warn'` is the default ("Forward only warnings and errors, by default"), but the AI blog post says only errors are forwarded by default — this is a minor conflict between the two official sources (see Conflicts section).

#### 3. Client-side setup required

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: No client-side setup is required. It is purely a `next.config.ts` flag. Next.js injects the forwarding mechanism automatically.
- **Quote**: "Next.js now forwards browser errors to the terminal by default during development, so you can see client-side errors without switching to the browser console." — https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai

#### 4. Relationship to `instrumentation-client.ts` / Sentry `captureConsoleIntegration`

- **Confidence**: Uncertain
- **Claim**: The official docs and blog post make no mention of `instrumentation-client.ts` or Sentry's `captureConsoleIntegration` interaction. There is no documented deduplication, conflict, or composition story. This is an open gap — both mechanisms intercept `console.*` at the client level, so double-firing or ordering issues are plausible but unverified against source code.
- **Provenance**: Not addressed in https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai or the logging docs reference page. Would require reading Next.js source or Sentry SDK source to resolve.

#### 5. AGENTS.md convention

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: `create-next-app` now scaffolds an `AGENTS.md` file by default in 16.2. It is a short directive telling agents to read docs from `node_modules/next/dist/docs/` before writing code. The Next.js npm package bundles full docs as Markdown. `CLAUDE.md` uses `@AGENTS.md` to include it as context for Claude Code. This is a separate feature from browser log forwarding — they are co-released but independent.
- **Quote**: "`create-next-app` now includes an `AGENTS.md` file by default, giving AI coding agents access to version-matched Next.js documentation from the start of your project." — https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai
- **Provenance**: Based on Vercel's own research showing 100% eval pass rate with bundled docs vs 79% with skill-based retrieval.

#### 6. Turbopack dependency / dev-only vs production

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: Browser log forwarding is dev-only. The docs explicitly say "during development" throughout. There is no Turbopack requirement stated — it lives under the `logging` key which is a general dev config. The `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal` predecessor similarly applied to dev mode. Production builds (`next start`) are not mentioned as supported.
- **Quote**: "This is useful for debugging client-side code without needing to check the browser's developer tools." (context: development section throughout the page) — https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/browserDebugInfoInTerminal

#### 7. Exact version

- **Confidence**: High
- **Claim**: Stable `logging.browserToTerminal` landed in **v16.2.0** exactly. The experimental predecessor `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal` was introduced in v15.4.0. No point release beyond 16.2.0 is noted as required.
- **Quote**: Version History table — "v16.2.0 — `browserToTerminal` added" — https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/browserDebugInfoInTerminal

#### 8. Known caveats / performance impact

- **Confidence**: Low
- **Claim**: No caveats or performance impact are documented in the official blog post or the logging docs reference page. This is an open gap.

---

### Source Chain

1. https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai — Official Next.js blog, AI-focused deep-dive, published 2026-03-18. Authority: official docs/announcement.
2. https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/browserDebugInfoInTerminal — Official Next.js config reference, includes version history table. Authority: official docs.
3. https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2 — Main release notes, confirms the AI blog exists and lists the feature. Authority: official docs.

---

### Conflicts

- **Default log level**: The AI blog post (https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai) states "only errors are forwarded to the terminal" by default. The logging docs reference page (https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/browserDebugInfoInTerminal) says `'warn'` is the default ("Forward only warnings and errors, by default"). The docs reference page is the more authoritative source for config behavior, but these two official sources directly contradict each other on the default value.

---

### Gaps

- Whether `logging.browserToTerminal` intercepts uncaught exceptions (unhandled promise rejections, `window.onerror`) or only explicit `console.*` calls — docs show only `console.*` examples, no exception mention.
- Whether full source-mapped stack traces are forwarded on errors, or only the single call-site line/column shown in the example.
- Interaction with Sentry's `captureConsoleIntegration` in `instrumentation-client.ts` — not addressed anywhere in official sources; requires reading the Next.js client injection code in the GitHub repo to resolve.
- No documented performance impact or warning about high-volume `true` mode in hot paths.
- The `@vercel/next-browser` CLI (`next-browser`) is described as experimental and separate from the config flag — it is not the same feature. It requires `npx skills add vercel-labs/next-browser` and manages a Chromium instance. Do not conflate the two.

### Local Relevance

- If the project is currently on Next.js 15.x, the flag to use is `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal`, not `logging.browserToTerminal`. The stable key only applies on 16.2.0+.
- The existing `CLAUDE.md` in this repo does not currently reference `AGENTS.md` or the `@AGENTS.md` directive pattern — adding that would activate the bundled docs feature once on 16.2.

Right — this is `logging.browserToTerminal`, stabilized in **16.2.0** (promoted from `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal`). You're on 16.2.4 so you can use the stable key directly. Nothing currently sets it in your configs.

## The flag

Add to `vendor/next/src/config.ts` so both apps inherit it:

```ts
logging: {
  browserToTerminal: true,   // forwards all console.log/info/warn/error
  // or 'warn' / 'error' / false
},
```

- No client-side setup. Purely a config flag, dev-only.
- Each forwarded line includes `(file:line:col)` source location.
- Shipped in Next 16.2.0 — version check: `pnpm why next` should show `^16.2.4` ✓.

## Gotcha to be aware of

**Potential dedup conflict with Sentry's `captureConsoleIntegration`.** In `apps/app/src/instrumentation-client.ts:51-53` and the main instrumentation file, you've got:

```ts
captureConsoleIntegration({ levels: ["error", "warn"] })
```

Both Next's forwarder and Sentry's integration patch `console.*` on the client. Neither doc mentions interaction behavior. In dev that means warn/error will likely show up in the terminal AND also hit Sentry via the Sentry SDK (since it still monkey-patches the originals). Not broken — but if you start seeing doubled stack traces in the terminal or noisy `extraErrorData` in Sentry dev events, that's why.

Safe mitigation: scope Sentry's `captureConsoleIntegration` to `NEXT_PUBLIC_VERCEL_ENV !== 'development'` if it becomes noisy. Don't do this preemptively — just know where to look.

## Caveats worth knowing

- **Dev-only.** `next start` / prod builds don't forward — Sentry is still your production path.
- **Default value conflict** between Next's own docs: the AI blog says default is "errors only", the config reference says `'warn'`. Set explicitly (`true` | `'warn'` | `'error'`) rather than relying on default.
- **Uncaught exceptions & full stack traces** aren't explicitly documented — official examples only show `console.*`. If you need unhandled-rejection coverage in the terminal, keep Sentry's client init for that.
- **Turbopack-agnostic** — works with both bundlers.

## While you're there — `AGENTS.md`

You've got `AGENTS.md` untracked at the repo root (I see it in git status). 16.2's `create-next-app` scaffolds this to point agents at `node_modules/next/dist/docs/` (Next bundles its own docs as MD in the npm package now). Worth filling in and referencing from `CLAUDE.md` with `@AGENTS.md` so Claude Code reads the version-matched Next docs instead of training data.

Separately, don't confuse `logging.browserToTerminal` with `@vercel/next-browser` — the latter is an experimental Chromium-driver CLI, different feature co-released in 16.2.

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Repository Overview

See `SPEC.md` for business goals and product vision.

**Lightfast** is a pnpm monorepo (Turborepo) for building AI agent orchestration tools.

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Next.js Apps                                                                   │
│  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌────────────────────────────────────┐                  │
│  │ app (4107)       │  │ www (4101)                         │                  │
│  │ @api/app     │  │ marketing + docs (fumadocs MDX)    │                  │
│  │ tRPC + Inngest   │  │ CMS                                │                  │
│  │ + auth routes    │  │                                    │                  │
│  └───────┬──────────┘  └────────────────────────────────────┘                  │
│          │                                                                      │
│  ┌──────────────────┐                                                          │
│  │ platform (4112)  │                                                          │
│  │ @api/platform      │  Connections, webhooks, backfill, neural pipeline        │
│  │ tRPC + Inngest   │  OAuth flows, token vault, event ingestion               │
│  └──────────────────┘                                                          │
│                          @db/app (Drizzle)                                  │
│                          @vendor/upstash (Redis)                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Packages: @repo/* (ui, lib, ai)  |  @repo/app-* (23)  |  @vendor/* (18)
```

### Vercel Microfrontends (lightfast.ai)

2 apps (app, www) served through single domain via `apps/app/microfrontends.json`.
App is default app (catch-all routes, sitemap.xml, robots.txt, auth routes).
Auth routes (/sign-in, /sign-up, /early-access) are served directly by app (migrated from former apps/auth).
Docs served via microfrontends mesh through `lightfast-www` (`/docs`, `/docs/:path*` routes in `apps/app/microfrontends.json`).

### Platform Service

Standalone Next.js app (`apps/platform`, port 4112) that consolidates the former relay, gateway, and backfill Hono microservices into a single tRPC + Inngest service.
Handles: OAuth flows, token vault, connection lifecycle, webhook ingestion, backfill orchestration, and neural pipeline (event capture → entity upsert → graph).
Domain: `platform.lightfast.ai`.

### tRPC Auth Boundaries
- **userRouter**: No org required (account, apiKeys, sources)
- **orgRouter**: Org membership required (workspace, search, jobs)

## Development Commands

```bash
# Dev servers (NEVER use global pnpm build)
pnpm dev:full         # Full stack: app + www + platform (port 3024 via microfrontends)
pnpm dev:app          # App only (4107)
pnpm dev:www          # Marketing + docs site (4101)
pnpm dev:platform     # Platform service (4112)

# Run dev server in background (for Claude Code sessions)
pnpm dev:app > /tmp/console-dev.log 2>&1 &
tail -f /tmp/console-dev.log  # Follow logs
pkill -f "next dev"           # Kill all dev servers

# Environment variables (MUST run from apps/<app>/ directory)
cd apps/app && pnpm with-env <command>

# Build & Quality
pnpm build:app                            # Next.js build
pnpm build:platform                       # Platform service build
pnpm check && pnpm typecheck

# Database (run from db/app/)
pnpm db:generate      # NEVER write manual .sql files
pnpm db:migrate
pnpm db:studio
```

Note: ngrok and inngest automatically runs with `pnpm dev:app`. You can test ngrok connection with `ps aux | grep ngrok | grep -v grep`

## Key Rules

1. **Vendor abstractions**: Standalone re-exports of third-party SDKs. Never import `@planetscale/*` directly → use `@vendor/db`
2. **Workspace protocol**: Use `workspace:*` for internal deps, `catalog:` for shared externals
3. **tRPC pattern**: `prefetch()` BEFORE `<HydrateClient>` to avoid UNAUTHORIZED errors
4. **Background jobs**: Inngest workflows in `api/app/src/inngest/workflow/` and `api/platform/src/inngest/`

## Environment

- **Node.js** >= 22.0.0 | **pnpm** 10.32.1 (pinned via `packageManager` in root `package.json` — that's the source of truth)
- **Env files**: `apps/<app>/.vercel/.env.development.local`

## Troubleshooting

```bash
pkill -f "next dev"                    # Port in use
pnpm clean:workspaces && pnpm install  # Module not found
pnpm --filter @api/app build       # tRPC type errors (api layer stays @api/app)
```

input.command:
gh pr create --title "chore(next): enable browserToTerminal log forwarding" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
- Enable Next 16.2's stable `logging.browserToTerminal` in the shared `@vendor/next` baseConfig
- Forwards client `console.log/info/warn/error` to the dev terminal with `(file:line:col)` source locations
- Both `apps/app` and `apps/platform` inherit it via `lodash.merge(baseConfig, …)`

## Why
Next 16.2.0 promoted this from `experimental.browserDebugInfoInTerminal` to a stable `logging.browserToTerminal` key. Makes in-editor AI agents (Claude Code, Cursor) able to see client-side errors without tab-switching to devtools. Dev-only — no effect on `next start` or prod builds.

## Notes
- Sentry's `captureConsoleIntegration({ levels: ["error", "warn"] })` in `apps/app/src/instrumentation-client.ts` also patches `console.*`. Both will fire in dev — benign, but expect warn/error to show up in terminal AND in Sentry dev client. Scope the Sentry integration to non-dev only if it gets noisy.
- Official docs conflict on the default value (`'warn'` vs `'error'`), so the flag is set explicitly to `true`.

## Test plan
- [ ] `pnpm dev:app` and trigger a `console.log` from a client component — verify it appears in terminal with `(path:line:col)`
- [ ] Same for `pnpm dev:platform`
- [ ] `pnpm build:app` + `pnpm build:platform` still succeed
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5
